caveats: a: The only detailed industry occupation table is NAICS 711100, not 711130.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and may not capture contractors consistently.
a: Observed generative-AI usage is not equivalent to safe substitution in rights-sensitive creative work.
rho: Neither implementation source studies musical groups or live-event operations.
rho: Customer-support productivity may not transfer to relationship-based booking or artistic workflows.
rho: Rights clearance, performer consent, and brand control may constrain otherwise feasible automation.
e: Dataset n is margin-bridged using a recreation-industry margin and may misclassify artist and nonprofit economics.
e: The published NAICS definition establishes heterogeneity but does not quantify the LMM mix.
e: A transferable brand or repertoire cannot compensate for customer demand tied to an excluded named artist.
s5: Published music transactions often concern catalogs, royalties, promoters, venues, or agencies rather than NAICS 711130 operating control.
s5: Personnel succession is not automatically a qualifying control transfer.
s5: Distress can dissolve a group instead of creating a transferable transaction.
q: Billing structures and bargaining power vary sharply by genre, venue, and artist recognition.
q: Live Nation is a global promoter, venue, and ticketing company, not an LMM musical group.
q: Retained benefit is not equivalent to owner cash flow in a nonprofit ensemble.
d5: BLS output covers theater and dance as well as music.
d5: The NEA comparison spans a pandemic disruption and is not a normalized trend.
d5: Live Nation's results are global, promoter-led, and disproportionately reflect major tours and large venues.
o: The estimate applies to today's live service basket, not recorded music or composition.
o: Virtual and synthetic acts may substitute faster for background entertainment and standardized event music than for concerts.
o: Operator-required demand can persist even when the artist brand is not transferable.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeated fixed-fee engagements, guarantees, door or ticket-share arrangements, self-produced ticketed performances, residencies, subscriptions, sponsorships, merchandise, and, for some orchestras, grants and contributions; delivery is project- or season-based rather than continuously contracted.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 711130 expressly combines musical groups with independent freelance artists and includes soloists as well as orchestras. The record narrows to ensemble operating companies because personality-dependent solo acts and transferable multi-person service organizations have fundamentally different control-transfer economics; the narrowing is for coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent solo musicians and vocalists, celebrity or bandleader vehicles whose earnings and customer demand are inseparable from one named person, nonprofit or public ensembles without a feasible qualifying control path, music promoters and agents, composition-only businesses, catalog or royalty investment vehicles, shells, and captive internal groups.
lens.included_activities: US ensemble operating companies in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly provide live musical performances to venues, presenters, event clients, ticket buyers, sponsors, and institutions, including transferable bands, orchestras, chamber groups, and multi-ensemble operators.

a.bridge: BLS measures headcount and wages for 4-digit Performing Arts Companies rather than 6-digit Musical Groups and Artists, while Anthropic measures actual AI use by broad occupation rather than feasible wage substitution in music ensembles. I map booking support, marketing, social media, customer response, finance, payroll, scheduling, grant or sponsor material, and parts of production planning to moderate or high exposure, but map instrumental or vocal performance, practice, rehearsal, load-in, and live artistic direction to low exposure. Because 711100 includes theater and dance and excludes self-employed workers, its occupation mix may overstate venue, hospitality, and production roles relative to the narrowed ensemble lens, pushing the estimate upward.
caveats: The only detailed industry occupation table is NAICS 711100, not 711130.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers and may not capture contractors consistently.
Observed generative-AI usage is not equivalent to safe substitution in rights-sensitive creative work.
a.change_evidence: A 711130 payroll and contractor-spend sample mapped to O*NET tasks, with time diaries from booking, finance, marketing, tour management, production, and performers at 20-30 LMM ensemble operators, would materially change the wage-weighted exposure.
a.rationale: The nearest industry staffing table shows management at 8.62% of employment, office and administrative support at 7.48%, and musicians and singers at 15.77%; the broader arts and media group is 41.79%. BLS describes musicians' core work as performance, audition, practice, and rehearsal, but also lists booking and career promotion. AI can materially assist the latter digital tasks and back office, while only a small share of the live musicianship itself is substitutable without changing the service.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for 4-digit Performing Arts Companies at 2.1% annually and musician employment at 1% over ten years, while NEA surveys US adults and Live Nation reports global, promoter-skewed large-event results. I translate these conflicting adjacent measures into a five-year constant-quality service-demand range centered below the roughly 1.11 five-year compounding of BLS's broad output rate. Live Nation's scale and international stadium growth likely bias demand upward for LMM US ensembles; pandemic-affected 2022 attendance likely biases classical and jazz demand downward if recovery continues.
caveats: BLS output covers theater and dance as well as music.
The NEA comparison spans a pandemic disruption and is not a normalized trend.
Live Nation's results are global, promoter-led, and disproportionately reflect major tours and large venues.
d5.change_evidence: US-only 2024-2026 paid attendance, show count, real guarantee, booking, and repeat-client data for sub-arena ensembles from Pollstar, Performing Arts Readiness or DataArts, booking platforms, and orchestra statistical reports would materially move the five-year ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects chained-dollar performing-arts-company output from $29.8 billion in 2024 to $36.9 billion in 2034, while musician and singer employment rises only 1% over the decade. The NEA found 21% of adults attended an unlisted live music, dance, or theater form in 2022, but jazz, Latin music, and classical-music attendance remained below 2017. More recently, Live Nation reported 159 million global concert fans in 2025, up 5%, and first-quarter 2026 ticket sales for its concerts up 11%. Together these support modest real growth with a substantial dispersion by genre and scale.

caveats: Dataset n is margin-bridged using a recreation-industry margin and may misclassify artist and nonprofit economics.
The published NAICS definition establishes heterogeneity but does not quantify the LMM mix.
A transferable brand or repertoire cannot compensate for customer demand tied to an excluded named artist.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 279 modeled LMM entities classifying legal form, performer concentration, revenue by named act, contract assignability, lineup continuity, customer recurrence, normalized EBITDA, and board or equity control would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition spans bands and orchestras but also independent musicians, vocalists, popular artists, and soloists. Even among employer firms in the modeled LMM band, many operations are economically inseparable from a named performer, leader, or current lineup; catalog or royalty assets do not by themselves make the live-service operation transferable. Orchestras may be nonprofit and therefore lack salable owner economics under 501(c)(3) rules. Eligibility is limited to operators with repeat external demand, replaceable or institutionalized talent, assignable customer relationships, and a feasible control path.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 711130 ($3,642,882,705) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 711130 ($7,487,328,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $196,384 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 711130); class <5: 4,263 firms, avg revenue/firm $481,476 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,118 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 609 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,582,287 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $263,293 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 310 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,570,858 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $594,191 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 198 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,915,116 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,150,675 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 81 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,934,272 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,984,263 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $90,391,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,041,146 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 279

caveats: The estimate applies to today's live service basket, not recorded music or composition.
Virtual and synthetic acts may substitute faster for background entertainment and standardized event music than for concerts.
Operator-required demand can persist even when the artist brand is not transferable.
o.change_evidence: US audience and event-buyer experiments comparing willingness to pay, repeat booking, satisfaction, and substitution for human live ensembles versus synthetic, virtual, or fully automated alternatives, split by event type and genre, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: The narrowed service basket is live musical performance. BLS describes performance, audition, practice, rehearsal, booking, and promotion, and Live Nation's 2026 filing explicitly reports rising demand for physically present live experiences. Generative music, virtual performances, backing tracks, automated booking, and self-service media can displace peripheral work or some low-stakes engagements, but the buyer of a live ensemble, residency, or concert still requires an accountable operator to assemble talent, secure rights, manage logistics, and deliver the performance.

caveats: Billing structures and bargaining power vary sharply by genre, venue, and artist recognition.
Live Nation is a global promoter, venue, and ticketing company, not an LMM musical group.
Retained benefit is not equivalent to owner cash flow in a nonprofit ensemble.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract repository covering guarantees, door splits, ticketed events, corporate and wedding engagements, residencies, sponsorships, and union agreements, paired with renewal pricing after measured automation savings, would materially tighten retention.
q.rationale: Fixed engagement fees, guarantees, ticket prices, sponsorships, and door splits do not usually reimburse internal administrative labor dollar for dollar, allowing initial retention of implemented savings. Over five years, presenters and event clients can renegotiate, competitors can price more aggressively, and musicians, managers, and contractors can capture part of the gain through compensation. Nonprofit orchestras may reinvest savings into mission and programming rather than distribute them. Live Nation's 2025 filing shows ticket and concert economics can grow with attendance, but its promoter and venue scale does not establish retention for LMM groups.

rho.bridge: Anthropic's cross-industry enterprise API observations and NBER's customer-support field deployment do not measure music ensembles. I apply their adoption and realized-productivity evidence only to exposed digital workflows, then discount for fragmented booking and ticketing systems, irregular engagements, contractor coordination, copyright and likeness review, brand sensitivity, and limited internal technology staff. Evidence from large or digitally mature organizations likely biases implementation upward for the LMM ensemble population.
caveats: Neither implementation source studies musical groups or live-event operations.
Customer-support productivity may not transfer to relationship-based booking or artistic workflows.
Rights clearance, performer consent, and brand control may constrain otherwise feasible automation.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after deployments at ensemble operators measuring staff hours, booking conversion, settlement errors, rights incidents, rework, and sustained use across CRM, finance, scheduling, and marketing would materially tighten this range.
rho.rationale: Anthropic reports that office-administrative tasks reached 13% of enterprise API transcripts in November 2025 and identifies email, document processing, CRM, and scheduling as automated workflows. Separately, a 5,179-agent field study found about 14% average productivity improvement from generative-AI assistance. Five years is enough to implement standardized booking intake, patron response, sponsor proposals, settlement preparation, schedule coordination, and content repurposing, but human approval remains important wherever reputation, rights, artistic voice, or live logistics are involved.

caveats: Published music transactions often concern catalogs, royalties, promoters, venues, or agencies rather than NAICS 711130 operating control.
Personnel succession is not automatically a qualifying control transfer.
Distress can dissolve a group instead of creating a transferable transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year transaction census using PitchBook, Grata, Capital IQ, state charity merger filings, Form 990 Schedule N, music-business legal advisers, and interviews with booking agencies and orchestra boards would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: No source found measures qualifying five-year control transfers for eligible LMM ensemble operators. Founder or manager retirement, partner buyouts, strategic combinations, and nonprofit mergers can create events, but performer departures, lineup changes, artist deaths, catalog purchases, and internal governance rotations do not necessarily transfer an ongoing controlled service firm. The bounded estimate therefore counts only transactions that preserve a transferable operating platform.

principal_driver: Repeat live-performance demand remains operator-required while booking and administrative workflows are increasingly automatable.
principal_weakness: Artist and lineup dependence, plus nonprofit governance, makes much of the apparent firm population non-transferable.

caveats: S4 supports a roughly 5,000-agent study rather than the record's exact 5,179-agent count; the productivity result itself is supported, so this precision issue does not change rho, H, the base tier, or the action.

The d5 rationale misstates S9's forward sales indicator as first-quarter 2026 concert ticket sales up 11%; the filing reports current 2026 ticket sales up 10%. Correcting this decorative statistic would not materially move d5 or D and would not change the base tier or action.

The central a, rho and d5 values rely on disclosed but distant proxies: four-digit performing-arts staffing and output, cross-industry Claude usage, customer-support productivity, pandemic-affected arts attendance, and global large-promoter results. Their PROXY labels and directional bridges are honest, but target-population evidence remains weak.

The eligibility, five-year transfer, retention and operator-required-demand inputs remain bounded judgments without target-specific transaction, contract or substitution data. This is reflected in ESTIMATE states, MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and a wide tier interval, so it warrants caution rather than rejection.

sources_audited.note: The Census definition directly supports the stated 711130 scope, illustrative groups and independent artists, and exclusions for promoters and independent composers or arrangers.
The May 2023 OEWS table supports the cited 711100 occupation shares and expressly states that estimates exclude self-employed workers; its broader four-digit population is disclosed in the bridge.

The report supports the November 2025 13% office-administrative API share, the three-percentage-point increase, the email/document/CRM/scheduling examples, and arts-related writing and editing usage. The extrapolation to ensemble operators is properly labeled PROXY.

The cited NBER digest supports a roughly 5,000-agent customer-support study and a 13.8%, or nearly 14%, increase in issues resolved per hour, but the exact 5,179 count stated in the record is not shown on the cited digest page.

The IRS page directly supports the prohibition on operation for private interests and private inurement for 501(c)(3) organizations. The implications for conventional owner economics are a reasonable, clearly stated inference rather than a measured eligibility or retention rate.
BLS supports live performance, practice, rehearsal, auditioning, promotion and career-development activities, common part-time work, and the 2024-2034 employment projection from 169,800 to 171,600.
The BLS industry table directly supports 711100 output rising from $29.8 billion in 2024 to $36.9 billion in 2034 at a 2.1% compound annual rate, and employment growth of 3.6%.

The NEA report supports the 21% other-live-performance attendance result and the cited 2022 jazz, Latin music and classical attendance rates and declines from 2017, as well as the venue and discovery observations.

The 2025 Form 10-K supports 159 million fans, 55,000 events, more than 11,000 artists, 5% fan growth, 10% concert-revenue growth, international and stadium concentration, and fixed-guarantee or revenue-share economics. It does not support the rationale's statement that first-quarter 2026 concert ticket sales were up 11%; it instead says current 2026 ticket sales were up 10% at the filing date.

sources.supports: Industry scope and heterogeneity: groups producing live musical entertainment and independent freelance artists, including bands, orchestras, popular artists, musicians, vocalists, and soloists; excludes promoters and independent composers or arrangers.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 711130 Musical Groups and Artists

sources.supports: Nearest industry occupation mix: management 8.62%, arts/design/entertainment/sports/media 41.79%, musicians and singers 15.77%, and office and administrative support 7.48%; the page notes estimates exclude self-employed workers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 711100 Performing Arts Companies

sources.supports: Observed November 2025 AI-use patterns, including office-administrative tasks at 13% of enterprise API transcripts after a 3 percentage-point rise, automation-oriented email, document, CRM, and scheduling workflows, and arts-related writing and editing use.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Economic primitives

sources.supports: Field evidence from 5,179 customer-support agents that AI assistance raised issues resolved per hour by nearly 14% on average, with larger effects for less-experienced workers.
sources.title: Measuring the Productivity Impact of Generative AI

sources.supports: Tax-exempt organizations must operate for exempt rather than private interests, and no earnings may inure to a private shareholder or individual, constraining conventional owner economics for nonprofit orchestras and ensembles.
sources.title: Exemption requirements - 501(c)(3) organizations

sources.supports: Musician duties spanning live performance, audition, practice, rehearsal, booking, and promotion; part-time work is common; and employment is projected to rise 1% from 169,800 in 2024 to 171,600 in 2034.
sources.title: Musicians and Singers: Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: BLS chained-2017-dollar projection for NAICS 711100: output rises from $29.8 billion in 2024 to $36.9 billion in 2034, a 2.1% compound annual rate; employment rises 3.6%.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry

sources.supports: In 2022, 21% of US adults attended an unlisted live music, dance, or theater form; jazz attendance was 6.3%, Latin music 3.9%, and classical music 4.6%, all below 2017; venue and discovery patterns also show bars, outdoor facilities, and social media importance.
sources.title: Arts Participation Patterns in 2022: Highlights from the Survey of Public Participation in the Arts

sources.supports: Global live-music demand proxy: 159 million fans attended 55,000 events involving more than 11,000 artists in 2025; fan count rose by 8 million or 5%, and concert revenue rose 10%, with growth concentrated in international markets and stadium content.
sources.title: Live Nation Entertainment 2025 Form 10-K

title: Musical Groups and Artists
